title: Ash-Smoked Carob Torte with Fig 'Ganache'
tagline: What chocolate cake became after the cacao extinction of 1998.
scenario: In 1989, witches' broom disease jumps quarantine from Bahia to a cargo hold bound for Abidjan. By 1994 it has crossed with frosty pod rot into a hybrid blight no fungicide touches, and West Africa — seventy percent of the world's cacao — loses its harvest three years running. The last commercial cacao pod is auctioned in Zurich in April 1998 for the price of a house. Confectionery does not die; it adapts. Carob, the Mediterranean's forgotten chocolate substitute, is suddenly planted from Cyprus to California, and a generation of pastry chefs works out how to give its date-like sweetness the darkness cacao once provided: fermented depth from aged barley miso, bitterness from burnt honey, smoke from resting the carob over smoldering tea leaves. The 'torta nera' emerges from Turin around 2003 — a dense, ash-scented torte glazed with a ganache of dried figs, since white 'chocolate' vanished too. Children born after the extinction insist, loyally, that it is better than whatever chocolate was.
scenarioSummary: 1998 — A hybrid fungal blight drives cacao extinct worldwide; pastry chefs rebuild 'chocolate' from smoked carob, dark miso and burnt honey.
decoys: 1973 — Cocoa-producing nations form a cartel and embargo the West; European bakers spend a decade perfecting substitutes.
2031 — The EU bans all cacao imports over deforestation, and a Piedmontese collective invents a compliant 'torta nera'.

items.item: burnt honey (140 g honey simmered dark, see step 2)
dark barley miso (hatcho or aka miso)
smoked sea salt, plus flakes to finish

method: Smoke the carob: line a dry lidded pot with foil, add the lapsang souchong, and set a steamer basket above with the 90 g carob powder spread on parchment. Heat until the leaves smolder, lid on, then smoke off the heat for 10 minutes. (Impatient timeline: whisk ½ tsp of the brewed tea, very strong, into the carob instead.)

Burn the honey: simmer 140 g honey in a saucepan, swirling, 4–6 minutes until it darkens to mahogany and smells faintly bitter. You need 120 g. Whisk in the butter off the heat, then the miso until smooth. Cool to warm.
Heat the oven to 165 °C (330 °F). Butter and line a 23 cm springform.
Whisk egg yolks with 50 g of the brown sugar until pale and ribbony, then whisk in the honey-miso-butter, smoked carob, almond flour, rice flour and salt. The batter will be thick.
Whip the egg whites with the remaining 50 g sugar to soft peaks. Loosen the batter with a third, then fold in the rest in two additions.
Bake 35–40 minutes, until the center barely trembles and a skewer comes out with moist crumbs. Cool fully in the tin; it will settle into a dense, fudgy crumb.

Fig ganache: cover the figs with boiling water for 10 minutes, drain, then blend hot with the coconut cream, carob, brandy and smoked salt until completely silky — 2 full minutes in a good blender, straining if needed.
Spread the ganache over the cooled torte in soft waves. Chill 1 hour. Serve in thin slices with smoked salt flakes; in this timeline, no one garnishes with chocolate shavings.

verification.verdict: No published recipe combines smoked carob, dark miso and burnt honey in a torte, nor a dried-fig ganache glaze. Carob desserts found (mousse, fudge, brownies) share no technique with this construction.
